abstract: The social sciences and humanities (SSH) traditionally have a close relationship to the nation-state and there are substantial disciplinary differences across countries. Drawing on Bourdieu's theory of the academic field, the present article examines how academic autonomy and heteronomy are applied as discursive strategies as the SSH compete for funding in the transnational European arena established by the European Research Council (ERC). To this end, we analyze mission statements of ERC Starting Grant projects in the SSH, using a mixed methods approach of statistical text analysis (topic modeling) and qualitative content analysis. Although the ERC puts the SSH under the constraints of academic capitalism, the classical humanities secure a strong position by signaling academic autonomy and engaging in the construction and consecration of European culture. However, economics and other social sciences gravitate toward a more heteronomous self-representation, emphasizing the political and social utility of their research, while disciplines like neuro-science and psychology exhibit self-representations closely related to the "hard sciences"-and relatively alien to the SSH.

paragraphs: There exists a peculiar relationship between the social sciences and humanities and the nation-state. The truism that scientific work is not conducted in a social vacuum, but is instead intimately tied to specific social forces and political powers, seems to be particularly true for these disciplines. The natural sciences have gravitated towards transnationalization and universalization since their very beginning, and can today be described as almost entirely globalized in content and structure. In contrast, the social sciences and humanities are to this day quite strongly moored to specific socio-political contexts, especially in nation-states or groups of states with a shared language and culture.

The present article examines what discursive effects emerge when the humanities and social sciences operate beyond such a strong anchoring in specific nation-state contexts. The opportunity to empirically pursue this question is presented by the relatively recent establishment of the European Research Council (ERC), a transnational funding body for "excellent frontier research", itself funded by the EU (Flink 2016;Hönig 2017). The ERC offers generous individual grants to researchers from all over the world as long as they are willing to conduct their research at an academic institution in the EU or one of its associated countries. In doing so, the ERC explicitly aims at creating a transnational "European Research Area", where the European frame of reference replaces the traditional national arena, with regard to K both funding and symbolic aspects. Hereinafter, we examine research projects in the Starting Grant category "Social Sciences and Humanities" that were funded in the first five years of the ERC's existence .

While most published research on the ERC and the European Research Area focuses on the specifics of the institutional setting, i. e. the rules of competition for grants, the composition of reviewing boards and panels etc. (e. g. EURECIA 2012; König 2017), the goal of the present article is to analyze the kind of academic discourse which emerges in these transnational, EU-governed settings. Therefore we analyze textual data-short summary texts-in which ERC-funded scientists present their work to the academic community as well as to the EU and domestic bureaucrats and politicians. Our analysis utilizes a mixed-methods approach, combining qualitative as well as statistical text analysis.

Before we provide further detail regarding our research goals, data, methods and theory, it seems appropriate to briefly discuss the special relationship between nation-states and the social sciences and humanities, and its role in establishing a system of academic governance and self-governance. Potentially, a similar process might be at play today as 'Europeanization'. Historically, since the beginning of 19th century, one major socio-political function of the humanities has been to canonize, legitimize, and thereby construct the "high culture" of nation states (Bourdieu 2014, p. 282). If nation-states are "imagined communities" (Anderson 2006(Anderson [1983]]), the humanities-especially in the late 18th and 19th century-helped to legitimize the idea of the nation as a "natural" entity and provided academic sanction for the cultural core of freshly established political formations such as the German Reich. As Gengnagel and Hamann (2014) argue, history became a major subject at German universities in the 19th century precisely because it equipped the "belated nation" with a legitimized, coherent narrative of its past and its culture. In general, universities in 19th century Europe were important breeding grounds and dissemination sites of nationalist thought (Hobsbawm 1996, p. 166), with the academic elite of the humanities leading the charge (Ringer 1990;Steinmetz 1999). The legacy of this intimate relationship between the humanities and the nation-state is still evident today, and it can even be argued that the idea of "nation" has become an integral part of the epistemological program of the humanities: Shumway (1998, p. 357) describes nationalism as one of the "unrecognized [...] rules of knowledge production" which guides academic practice in the humanities, even in disciplines where the object of study is not segregated nationally.

Similar trends-often discussed as "methodological nationalism"-can be observed in other disciplines such as sociology, political science and economics (cp. Beck 2007). In disciplines that have developed methodologies more akin to the natural sciences, e. g. economics and psychology, methodological nationalism seems primarily absent today. However, it is important to remember that the seemingly apolitical, universalistic position these disciplines adopt today may have, at least in part, been reached by a largely unrecognized universalization of certain national or European stereotypes (cp. Shumway 1998, p. 358). All in all, it seems fair to say that the historically close relationship between the social sciences and humanities and the nation-state has left deep traces in the SSH and their concepts of academic autonomy (Gengnagel et al. 2016b). Even in the more rigorously scientific disci-plines, a peculiar affinity for political positioning and an enduring difficulty to hold socially unbiased positions can be observed.

With Bourdieu, these structures can be described along the dimension of academic autonomy and heteronomy. Academic autonomy, as conceived by Bourdieu, is a type of academic self-image which exists in those areas where the academic field (purportedly) "governs itself", so that scholars are bound mainly to the purely scientific demands of the field itself. Positions of heteronomy are marked by an overt orientation toward-and an acknowledged dependence on-agents and issues outside the academic realm, for example the political, bureaucratic, economic or cultural field. According to the "logic of practice" (Bourdieu 1990), the particular rules which guide practice in social fields are never pure, but emerge from an ongoing, messy conflict between internal and external forces, each one pushing for dominance and autonomy.

Therefore, claims and signals of academic autonomy can only be understood against the backdrop of power relations which exist in the academic field. As in the field of art (Bourdieu 1996), signaling autonomy is in itself a powerful resource and as such subject of struggles for symbolic domination. Academic power can be understood in Bourdieu's terms as the ability to legitimately define and dictate the distinctions which organize academic practice, for example where the borders between disciplines lie or what counts as "scientific excellence". Thus, the question of academic autonomy points to strategic aspects of scientific practice such as selfpresentation, position-taking and self-legitimization, while the question of power addresses the structural and institutional inequality, which enables certain agents to impose their interests on the field (Bourdieu 1992;1998).

It follows that legitimate markers of academic autonomy are defined by those in power, and signaling academic autonomy or heteronomy are two opposing strategies which agents employ to get ahead in the power struggle, mobilizing both academic and non-academic claims to excellence. Because the present article aims to examine the manifest discursive effects of the ERC in the struggle for academic domination, we focus on those strategies of autonomy and heteronomy, and our objective is to illustrate how these strategies function in this specific transnational context. While we do not address the question in how far scientific practices de facto "govern themselves", we nevertheless refer to the underlying structures of power and domination in order to interpret how the observed strategies of self-representation emerge and what they are aiming at.

Departing from the assumption that a successful ERC starting grant application is an act of consecration to such a position of power, we examine the public representation of ERC-funded projects. After all, ERC research is supposed to become an academic "gold standard" (Nowotny 2013). The various indications of academic autonomy and heteronomy found in this discourse should be regarded as manifestations and preliminary results of ongoing academic power struggles. These struggles have been historically shaped by the nation state and thus have taken on a specific form and structure, which are now iterated and transformed in a transnational context.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: The aim of this paper is to ask how successful ERC starting grant applications employ strategies of symbolizing academic autonomy and heteronomy in their selfrepresentation, and how these specific strategies are shaped by the rules and power relations of the transnational ERC arena in which they take place: How is the conflict between academic autonomy and heteronomy in the SSH played out on the transnational scale-and does it relate to the encompassing power structure of Europe? Tackling these questions informs us of the role of academia in shaping Europe-and how Europeanization shapes academia in the process.

The article is structured as follows: In Sect. 2, the emerging transnational arena of the European Research Council (ERC) is described, using a field-theoretical perspective on Europeanization. In Sect. 3, we give details on our data and methods. Sect. 4 presents results from our statistical text analysis of ERC Starting Grant project summaries. Sect. 5 builds on these findings by investigating specific strategies of academic power and autonomy through a combination of qualitative and quantitative methods. In Sect. 6, we offer a brief discussion of our main results.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
sections.header_raw: The ERC and the europeanization of the academic field
sections.header_norm: the erc and the europeanization of the academic field
paragraphs: Currently, several Bourdieu-inspired contributions conceptualize Europeanization as a particular form of transnationalization (for an overview cp. Gengnagel 2014). Along with the political process of European Integration, an increasing interrelatedness of social groups is observed in and around the "Brussels Bubble"-the core of the European bureaucratic field (Georgakakis and Rowell 2013). Europeanization, in this perspective, becomes observable when social groups-e. g. juridical (Vauchez and De Witte 2013), economic (Lebaron 2000) or political (Kauppi 2013;Kauppi and Madsen 2013) elites-establish relatively binding rules across countries. These emerging transnational institutions can be in accordance with those of certain national arenas, or can oppose them: In cases of less conformity, the transnational field acts as an intrusive force in the national arena and may therefore be met with fierce resistance on the national level. This has consequences for the meaning of "Europeanization" in various social fields: Transnational European fields align themselves well with national fields that are in line with the EU economic logic of marketization, while fields with a strong national identity have more trouble being 'both European and national'. Accordingly, the antagonism between affirmative pro-European and eurosceptic positions is prevalent in all social fields, and especially virulent in more prestigious fields-politics, media, science, culture-which harbor enough symbolic capital to "lend authority" to the European project (Gengnagel et al. 2016a).

This latent line of conflict can also be traced across the academic field. Here, EU research policy is informed by the paradigm of academic capitalism (Slaughter and Rhoades 2004;Münch 2014). Academic capitalism conceptualizes scientific practice as an entrepreneurial activity and concludes that science functions best in a context of intense, sustained competition, with minimal political interference. Accordingly, governance of science should be based on the evaluation of output and efficiency alone, and should benefit those who perform best. EU science policy has largely embraced academic capitalism in an attempt at "reverse engineering Anglo-American higher education models" (Slaughter and Cantwell 2012) in order to intensify competition in the European academic landscape. The push for a "European Research Area", calling for European competitive dynamics that challenge both national academic traditions and the global hegemony of US research (Nedeva 2013), may serve as an example. A major point of criticism made by Slaughter, Münch and others pertains to how academic capitalism shifts the core values of scientific practice from the pursuit of knowledge and recognition among peers towards the pursuit of money and status ("excellence"). Such a shift in the self-image of researchers lies in the scope of the present article: Under the pressure of fierce competition in a transnational arena, new academic identities may emerge, which can be detected in the prevailing forms of discourse.

Historically, the EU made its first attempt at research policy through the so-called Framework Programmes, which were aimed at creating large transnational scientific enterprises in areas chosen by the EU Commission and usually with a strong focus on economic or social utility. However, the latent conflict between academic autonomy and heteronomy meant that the mission-oriented Framework Programmes, with their emphasis on societal impact over "pure" academic value, were only able to draw the interest of a minority of academics, and were largely ignored by the more prestigious academic elites, especially in the SSH (cp. Massih-Tehrani et al. 2015).

In 2007, however, this began to change with the European Research Council (ERC) becoming a central part of the EU's effort to construct a European Research Area. 1 The policy of the ERC differs from the openly mission-oriented Framework Programmes in at least three crucial points: The research topics funded are not pre-determined politically, but emerge bottom-up through the process of application and review. Additionally, the narrow focus on applied research and large, international collaborative networks is replaced by a focus on funding "excellent" individual scientists who carry out "frontier research". Furthermore, the ERC is directed by a committee of world-renowned scientists-the scientific council-which is in charge of developing guidelines and policies for the evaluation of proposals and the distribution of ERC grants (König 2017). With this type of strategy, the ERC establishes a funding scheme which pays more respect to the specificities and traditions that guide academic practice, and therefore resonates more strongly with researchers who focus on academic autonomy (Luukkonen 2014;Chessa et al. 2013;cf. ERC 2015). Because of the leading role of eminent scientists, the more autonomy-oriented academic elites are able to relate to the ERC as a European institution capable of consecrating what they accept as academic excellence (Hönig 2014).

A further major difference between the ERC and other forms of EU research policy is that the social sciences and the humanities are actively encouraged to participate in the enterprise. While the Framework Programmes are not that attractive for many scholars in these disciplines, the ERC has established a line of funding explicitly dedicated to the humanities and the social sciences. Additionally, the SSH 1 Other ways in which a certain "Europeanness" has been promoted in academic fields include the establishment of the European University Institute in Florence, Jean-Monnet-chairs (cf. Calligaro 2013, pp. 15-77) and the European Central Bank (Mudge and Vauchez 2016), amongst others.

These emerging transnational institutions can be in accordance with those of certain national arenas, or can oppose them: In cases of less conformity, the transnational field acts as an intrusive force in the national arena and may therefore be met with fierce resistance on the national level. This has consequences for the meaning of "Europeanization" in various social fields: Transnational European fields align themselves well with national fields that are in line with the EU economic logic of marketization, while fields with a strong national identity have more trouble being 'both European and national'. Accordingly, the antagonism between affirmative pro-European and eurosceptic positions is prevalent in all social fields, and especially virulent in more prestigious fields-politics, media, science, culture-which harbor enough symbolic capital to "lend authority" to the European project (Gengnagel et al. 2016a).

This latent line of conflict can also be traced across the academic field. Here, EU research policy is informed by the paradigm of academic capitalism (Slaughter and Rhoades 2004;Münch 2014). Academic capitalism conceptualizes scientific practice as an entrepreneurial activity and concludes that science functions best in a context of intense, sustained competition, with minimal political interference. Accordingly, governance of science should be based on the evaluation of output and efficiency alone, and should benefit those who perform best. EU science policy has largely embraced academic capitalism in an attempt at "reverse engineering Anglo-American higher education models" (Slaughter and Cantwell 2012) in order to intensify competition in the European academic landscape. The push for a "European Research Area", calling for European competitive dynamics that challenge both national academic traditions and the global hegemony of US research (Nedeva 2013), may serve as an example. A major point of criticism made by Slaughter, Münch and others pertains to how academic capitalism shifts the core values of scientific practice from the pursuit of knowledge and recognition among peers towards the pursuit of money and status ("excellence"). Such a shift in the self-image of researchers lies in the scope of the present article: Under the pressure of fierce competition in a transnational arena, new academic identities may emerge, which can be detected in the prevailing forms of discourse.

Historically, the EU made its first attempt at research policy through the so-called Framework Programmes, which were aimed at creating large transnational scientific enterprises in areas chosen by the EU Commission and usually with a strong focus on economic or social utility. However, the latent conflict between academic autonomy and heteronomy meant that the mission-oriented Framework Programmes, with their emphasis on societal impact over "pure" academic value, were only able to draw the interest of a minority of academics, and were largely ignored by the more prestigious academic elites, especially in the SSH (cp. Massih-Tehrani et al. 2015).

In 2007, however, this began to change with the European Research Council (ERC) becoming a central part of the EU's effort to construct a European Research Area. 1 The policy of the ERC differs from the openly mission-oriented Framework Programmes in at least three crucial points: The research topics funded are not pre-determined politically, but emerge bottom-up through the process of application and review. Additionally, the narrow focus on applied research and large, international collaborative networks is replaced by a focus on funding "excellent" individual scientists who carry out "frontier research". Furthermore, the ERC is directed by a committee of world-renowned scientists-the scientific council-which is in charge of developing guidelines and policies for the evaluation of proposals and the distribution of ERC grants (König 2017). With this type of strategy, the ERC establishes a funding scheme which pays more respect to the specificities and traditions that guide academic practice, and therefore resonates more strongly with researchers who focus on academic autonomy (Luukkonen 2014;Chessa et al. 2013;cf. ERC 2015). Because of the leading role of eminent scientists, the more autonomy-oriented academic elites are able to relate to the ERC as a European institution capable of consecrating what they accept as academic excellence (Hönig 2014).

A further major difference between the ERC and other forms of EU research policy is that the social sciences and the humanities are actively encouraged to participate in the enterprise. While the Framework Programmes are not that attractive for many scholars in these disciplines, the ERC has established a line of funding explicitly dedicated to the humanities and the social sciences. Additionally, the SSH 1 Other ways in which a certain "Europeanness" has been promoted in academic fields include the establishment of the European University Institute in Florence, Jean-Monnet-chairs (cf. Calligaro 2013, pp. 15-77) and the European Central Bank (Mudge and Vauchez 2016), amongst others.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: ERC Starting Grants support excellent researchers in the early years of their career and can therefore be seen as a significant element of the ERC's attempt at shaping the future of science in Europe-which is why the successful ERC Starting Grants provide the empirical basis for our study. The data we use for our analysis consist of 365 short summary texts which describe research projects funded through ERC Starting Grants in the category "SSH" (Social sciences and Humanities) between 2007 and 2012. These summaries are typically around 200-300 words long and contain descriptions of the given research project regarding its objectives, methods and expected outcomes, its position in the current development of the discipline, its expected societal impacts, etc.

As our analysis rests exclusively on these data, it is important to emphasize the specific context from which they emerge: Although initially the project summaries look much like abstracts of articles in research journals, they should not be thought of as elements of a purely academic discourse; rather, they are attempts to communicate scientific ideas and motivations in a way that is tailored to the bureaucratic requirements of the ERC application process. 2 Given their specific context and use, these summaries must be written so that they can be understood by multiple audiences, including ERC evaluators, fellow researchers, as well as non-experts like EU 2 One of these requirements is to situate the research project in the ERC's structure of evaluation panels. The ERC's peer review process operates with a nested structure of panels, each covering a number of academic disciplines and sub-disciplines. The top-level category "SSH" is made up of six sub-categories of panels which relate to broad disciplinary groups in the social sciences and humanities, e. g. "The Study of the Human Past", "The Human Mind and Its Complexity" or "Individuals, Institutions and Markets". Each of these is in turn subdivided into eleven to fourteen more specific sub-categories, e. g. "Macroeconomics", "International trade" and "Banking, corporate finance, accounting" in the aforementioned panel "Individuals, Institutions and Markets". Of course, this structure of thematic review panels influences the way in which the research summaries are written, and might also have a bearing on the prevalence and clustering of research topics. In the context of academic autonomy discussed here, it would be interesting to see how closely funded projects adhere to the formal structures of the ERC's application procedure and whether specific projects or disciplines differ in this regard. Unfortunately, our data does not allow us to compare the panel structure to the statistically constructed research topics in a systematic manner. Nevertheless, our more inductive approach enables us to identify traces of these structural contexts as well as differentials of strategic compliance in the project summaries.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: and national bureaucrats or politicians. The ERC offers the following guidelines for these summaries:

The abstract should, at a glance, provide the reader with a clear understanding of the objectives of the research proposal and how they will be achieved. The abstract will be used as the short description of your research proposal in the evaluation process and in communications to contact in particular the ERC experts and/or inform the Commission and/or the programme management committees and/or relevant national funding agencies [...] (ERCEA 2017, p. 14).

Because of the need to address multiple audiences-evaluators, fellow academics, bureaucrats, politicians-the project summaries are often much more emphatic about the benefits of their research as is characteristic of academic discourse. This divergence is a typical result of academic capitalism, where scientists are molded into entrepreneurs and therefore tend to engage in more aggressive forms of marketing (cp. Münch 2014, p. 65). The project summaries should be read with a focus on how they relate to traditional, established discursive forms in the SSH. They are, according to our theory, means of positioning and self-presentation in the academic power struggle, shaped by the ERC arena and its competing academic, bureaucratic and political imperatives. As we will see, various such strategies emerge in the competition for ERC funding "excellence"-and even in the successful ERC-starting grant projects, which are supposed to represent "frontier research", patterns of relative heteronomy can be discerned.

These data were collected in 2013 and 2014 from the CORDIS websitefoot_0 where the EU Commission offers information about all its activities in research funding to the general public. In a qualitative analysis of these project summaries, we collected further data on a number of issues, including how projects position themselves regarding questions of multi-, inter-and transdisciplinarity, whether they align themselves with a specific discipline, and whether they explicitly promise specific societal impacts of their undertaking.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: The project summaries are analyzed both qualitatively and quantitatively. In order to make them usable for statistical text analysis, we transformed the corpus into a document-term matrix, which lists all the words that occur in all the documents as variables and indicates the number of each word in each document. This initial matrix contained large amounts of redundant information, which we eliminated in various steps. These are standard procedures in statistical text analysis which are used to reduce the data to a manageable size and minimize bias from outlier cases. For example, we deleted so-called "stopwords", i. e. words like "and", "while", "in" etc., which are extremely common and therefore cannot be used for the characterization of documents. On the other end of the spectrum, we eliminated all words which occurred in less than four of our documents. We also converted all words to lowercase characters and removed punctuation. Our final data set contains word frequencies for 2074 words (variables) across N = 365 project summaries (cases).

The main statistical method we use for the analysis of this data set is called topic modeling. Topic models are "algorithms for discovering the main themes that pervade a large or otherwise unstructured collection of documents" (Blei 2012, p. 77). In a topic model, the words in the documents are analyzed in order to find an "inferred hidden structure" of topics (ibid. 79). Topics in this statistical sense are defined as probability distributions over a given set of words. For example, if we want to speak about classical music and have all the words in the Oxford English Dictionary at our disposal, the probability for using words like "violin" or "horn" is much higher than for many others. The overall number of topics in a given corpus is assumed as fixed and has to be specified beforehand. Furthermore, topic models assume that documents are composed of multiple topics, which vary in prominence. Thus, the model assumes that authors compose their texts by first choosing topics from a fixed set, and then selecting words from a given vocabulary to talk about these topics. Topic modeling attempts to reverse-engineer this process and to infer the latent topics which explain how the observed texts emerged from the observed vocabulary.

Topic models have been used to analyze huge collections of scientific texts (see for example Griffiths and Steyvers 2004). While there are various forms of topic modeling available today (see again Blei 2012 for an overview), we use Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), the simplest and most basic approach to topic modeling, as implemented in the R package "topicmodels" (Grün and Hornik 2011).

For our analysis, it is important to stress that because of how topic models are constructed, statistical correlations usually exist between topics, i. e. some topics, while distinguishable, occur side by side more often than others. Since such correlations are of interest in our approach, we use principle component analysis (PCA) to examine them and display them spatially.

Furthermore, we employ the qualitative approach of content analysis (Mayring 2000) in order to identify signs of disciplinary positioning, academic and societal impact, and references to Europe and the EU. The goal of this analysis was to validate the results from our statistical analysis and inform the interpretation of the Topic Model and PCA dimensions. Additionally, content analysis yields illustrative examples for the various strategies of self-representation employed in the ERCfunded SSH discourse (see Sect. 5).

For our analysis, it is important to stress that because of how topic models are constructed, statistical correlations usually exist between topics, i. e. some topics, while distinguishable, occur side by side more often than others. Since such correlations are of interest in our approach, we use principle component analysis (PCA) to examine them and display them spatially.

Furthermore, we employ the qualitative approach of content analysis (Mayring 2000) in order to identify signs of disciplinary positioning, academic and societal impact, and references to Europe and the EU. The goal of this analysis was to validate the results from our statistical analysis and inform the interpretation of the Topic Model and PCA dimensions. Additionally, content analysis yields illustrative examples for the various strategies of self-representation employed in the ERCfunded SSH discourse (see Sect. 5).

The project summaries are analyzed both qualitatively and quantitatively. In order to make them usable for statistical text analysis, we transformed the corpus into a document-term matrix, which lists all the words that occur in all the documents as variables and indicates the number of each word in each document. This initial matrix contained large amounts of redundant information, which we eliminated in various steps. These are standard procedures in statistical text analysis which are used to reduce the data to a manageable size and minimize bias from outlier cases. For example, we deleted so-called "stopwords", i. e. words like "and", "while", "in" etc., which are extremely common and therefore cannot be used for the characterization of documents. On the other end of the spectrum, we eliminated all words which occurred in less than four of our documents. We also converted all words to lowercase characters and removed punctuation. Our final data set contains word frequencies for 2074 words (variables) across N = 365 project summaries (cases).

The main statistical method we use for the analysis of this data set is called topic modeling. Topic models are "algorithms for discovering the main themes that pervade a large or otherwise unstructured collection of documents" (Blei 2012, p. 77). In a topic model, the words in the documents are analyzed in order to find an "inferred hidden structure" of topics (ibid. 79). Topics in this statistical sense are defined as probability distributions over a given set of words. For example, if we want to speak about classical music and have all the words in the Oxford English Dictionary at our disposal, the probability for using words like "violin" or "horn" is much higher than for many others. The overall number of topics in a given corpus is assumed as fixed and has to be specified beforehand. Furthermore, topic models assume that documents are composed of multiple topics, which vary in prominence. Thus, the model assumes that authors compose their texts by first choosing topics from a fixed set, and then selecting words from a given vocabulary to talk about these topics. Topic modeling attempts to reverse-engineer this process and to infer the latent topics which explain how the observed texts emerged from the observed vocabulary.

paragraphs: The document-term-matrix used for the topic model can be descriptively analyzed in order to provide some insight into the type of discourse we are dealing with. After removing redundant information, cases in our data set contain 63.3 words on average, with a standard deviation of 13.5 words. The most frequent words, which occur in more than 60 of the 365 documents, are: case, change, cultural, economic, empirical, europe, european, international, policy, political, social Among the 340 most infrequent words, which occur in only four documents, are for example: algorithms, colonies, emotions, freedom, genes, interface, paris, quantification, sweden, variable, water

We see that issues of policy, culture, society and Europe are at the center of the discourse examined here, while at its periphery, we find words associated with other scientific disciplines, like "algorithms" and "genes", as well as references to specific countries ("sweden") or cities ("paris"). Therefore, the discourse at hand obviously concentrates on classical topics of the social sciences and humanities, but combines them quite prominently with questions about Europe and the EU.

paragraphs: One challenge of constructing topic models, at least with the LDA approach employed here, lies in the fact that the number of topics has to be specified in advance. While there exist statistical measures which describe how well a given model fits the data, we argue that figuring out the right number of topics is as much a question of theory-driven interpretation as it is of model fitness. In the field of machine learning-the original context of topic models-questions of model fitness are predominant, because here, the task of topic models is to introduce some kind of structure into massive text corpora, and thereby provide orientation for human users. For example, Griffiths and Steyvers (2004) constructed a topic model of more than 28,000 abstracts of scientific papers. In contrast, we are dealing with a comparatively small corpus of 365 documents and we are interested in uncovering a structure which enables us to answer specific research questions. Therefore, we computed various topic models with topic numbers varying between 5 and 50. Considering our task here, we chose a model with 20 topics, which is detailed enough to represent the basic structure of the discourse, and sparse enough to enable an in-depth interpretation of the topics and their relations.

Fig. 1 shows the 20 topics, ranked by prominence across the 365 documents. The topics are labeled by the three words with the highest probability for each topic. For example, the most prominent topic is characterized by the words "century", "history", and "texts". Fig. 1 reveals important insights into the character of ERC-funded research:

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: Research regarding historical topics is most prominent, with research about the brain and cognition as a close second. Other important topics include the culture and history of Europe, financial markets, health, languages, as well as international law and global policy. It is equally instructive to see which topics are less important in this discourse: For example, a topic dealing with questions of religion (and their multiplicity) ranks quite low on the scale. Similarly, while "social" is one of the most common words in the corpus, the topic which specifically deals with societies as social issues is on the second to last ranking position. We can take this as an indication that sociology as a discipline is not very prominent in the context of the ERC, although social issues are discussed very frequently.

If we interpret these findings as manifestations and results of an ongoing struggle for academic power and prestige, we can see that certain disciplines seem to be presented more dominantly than others in the context of the ERC: The two major themes-history and the brain-point towards the disciplines of history and (neuro-)psychology as major players, while the other high-ranking topics belong to political science, economics and related fields like health and demography; other topics, e. g. those dealing with religion, social practices and societies occupy more peripheral positions, which indicates that disciplines like anthropology, theology and sociology are relatively weak in the ERC context.

With regard to the question of academic autonomy, it is interesting to notice the polar structure predicted by our theory: On the one hand, there seems to be a substantial amount of "pure" basic research into history and neuroscience, while K on the other hand, quite a lot of topics deal with politically charged issues like financial markets, global policy, health or international law.

paragraphs: As shown in Fig. 1, meaningful associations exist among some of the topics. For example, the first and third topic both deal with history and culture. In order to examine connections like these in a more systematic fashion, we employ Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to construct a latent topic space which is structured by the correlations among the 20 topics. In this approach, topics are regarded as similar if they appear together in multiple project summaries.

Fig. 2 shows a diagram of the first two dimensions of the PCA. Active variables (displayed in lower case) are the twenty topics obtained through LDA. Additionally, we employed a number of passive variables (displayed in upper case) which describe the disciplinary self-classification that we identified in the project summaries via content analysis. While such self-classifications are present only in a minority of the summaries, the passive variables help validate and further detail the findings of our topic model.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: To develop our analysis one step further, we look for clusters of topics that are very closely related statistically as well as semantically. In this way, we hope to further clarify the latent structure of the discourse under study and identify certain "poles" of meaning, around which the various research topics revolve.

On the left hand side of Fig. 2, there are two topics concerned with economics, markets and finance, which can be combined into a cluster entitled "Markets & Finance". Similarly, the three topics concerned with transnational politics, law and governance (located in the top central area of Fig. 2) are combined into a cluster labeled "Transnational Politics & Institutions". Furthermore, we join the two topics dealing with history on the far right of Fig. 2 into the cluster "History & Culture" as well as the topics dealing with psychological testing and brain imaging into the cluster "Psychology & Neuroscience". Thus, four topic-clusters emerge, and each one of them marks one of the semantic poles of our topic space.

By considering the polarity of research topics in this way, we recognize a logic of distinction into academic paradigms and disciplines behind the distributions of individual topics, which in itself is bound up with issues of academic autonomy and power. Thus, we observe that Economics-a relatively heteronomous discipline, focused on creating politically useful insights-is located on the far left of the topic space, where research into the economy and financial markets is prevalent, while Political Science, another relatively heteronomous discipline, is located in the top region, where research is mainly concerned with questions of transnational government, law and politics. Correspondingly, we can tentatively identify the location of two disciplines-history and (neuro)-psychology-which stand for relatively high academic autonomy: History is located on the right side of Fig. 2, while psychology is positioned at the bottom. Thus it appears that academic autonomy increases towards the right as well as towards the lower region of the topic space.

Having developed a basic understanding of the latent lines of academic struggle which underlie our topic space, we can now ask how the individual Starting Grant projects are positioned in this space. As shown in Fig. 3, the four polar research categories that characterize our topic space are still clearly discernable if we look at the individual projects. But we also see that no hard boundaries exist between these categories. Only the cluster "Psychology & Neuroscience" is strongly segregated from all the other topics in our data set, while the other three categories overlap significantly. This comes as no surprise, since it could be argued that psychology, with its contemporary objectivist-materialist paradigm embodied in "Neuroscience", is in many ways a foreign element in the sphere of the SSH. Nevertheless, in the context of the ERC these disciplines intrude upon the traditional turf of the SSH and have even become one of the most important factions in this arena. This intrusion can be interpreted as a shift in the power relations between disciplines, which characterizes the specific transnational setting of the ERC. In their national context, the SSH tend to be protected from such developments by layers of tradition and conservative academic institutions, while in the full-on, context-free competition engendered by EU's academic capitalism, an actual struggle seems to have broken out between the classical SSH-disciplines on the one hand and disciplines such as Neuroscience on the other hand. In order to gain further insight into these dynamics, we need to adopt a more sophisticated idea of what autonomy means, and how it is related to power relations in the context of field theory. Here we turn to the work of Schmitz et al. (2017), where a theoretical distinction is introduced between various types of dependence and independence from agents and resources inside as well as outside the given field. The logic of one field can act as a source of heteronomy in another, as seen, for example, in the case of market-oriented art or publicity-oriented politics. Because there are many social fields which interact in this way, various forms of autonomy K and heteronomy can exist. These need to be analyzed with the "field of power" in mind. With this concept, Bourdieu points to the overarching power structure in which all social fields are embedded and where each field occupies a more or less dominant or dominated position. The point here is that the struggle between autonomy and heteronomy inside a given field can only be understood if it is analyzed against this broader background of power relations inside as well as outside the field. We can apply these ideas for our purposes by 1. inquiring from whom/from what autonomous agents signal independence, and 2. by remembering that the discourse we examine is a result of strategies in an ongoing struggle for academic domination, which takes place in the context of various social fields, some of which are more powerful than others.
Even in the most autonomous academic practices, therefore, relative heteronomies remain among the structuring principles. They are also prevalent in the formulation of ERC Starting Grant summaries.

To develop our analysis one step further, we look for clusters of topics that are very closely related statistically as well as semantically. In this way, we hope to further clarify the latent structure of the discourse under study and identify certain "poles" of meaning, around which the various research topics revolve.

On the left hand side of Fig. 2, there are two topics concerned with economics, markets and finance, which can be combined into a cluster entitled "Markets & Finance". Similarly, the three topics concerned with transnational politics, law and governance (located in the top central area of Fig. 2) are combined into a cluster labeled "Transnational Politics & Institutions". Furthermore, we join the two topics dealing with history on the far right of Fig. 2 into the cluster "History & Culture" as well as the topics dealing with psychological testing and brain imaging into the cluster "Psychology & Neuroscience". Thus, four topic-clusters emerge, and each one of them marks one of the semantic poles of our topic space.

By considering the polarity of research topics in this way, we recognize a logic of distinction into academic paradigms and disciplines behind the distributions of individual topics, which in itself is bound up with issues of academic autonomy and power. Thus, we observe that Economics-a relatively heteronomous discipline, focused on creating politically useful insights-is located on the far left of the topic space, where research into the economy and financial markets is prevalent, while Political Science, another relatively heteronomous discipline, is located in the top region, where research is mainly concerned with questions of transnational government, law and politics. Correspondingly, we can tentatively identify the location of two disciplines-history and (neuro)-psychology-which stand for relatively high academic autonomy: History is located on the right side of Fig. 2, while psychology is positioned at the bottom. Thus it appears that academic autonomy increases towards the right as well as towards the lower region of the topic space.

Having developed a basic understanding of the latent lines of academic struggle which underlie our topic space, we can now ask how the individual Starting Grant projects are positioned in this space. As shown in Fig. 3, the four polar research categories that characterize our topic space are still clearly discernable if we look at the individual projects. But we also see that no hard boundaries exist between these categories. Only the cluster "Psychology & Neuroscience" is strongly segregated from all the other topics in our data set, while the other three categories overlap significantly. This comes as no surprise, since it could be argued that psychology, with its contemporary objectivist-materialist paradigm embodied in "Neuroscience", is in many ways a foreign element in the sphere of the SSH. Nevertheless, in the context of the ERC these disciplines intrude upon the traditional turf of the SSH and have even become one of the most important factions in this arena. This intrusion can be interpreted as a shift in the power relations between disciplines, which characterizes the specific transnational setting of the ERC. In their national context, the SSH tend to be protected from such developments by layers of tradition and conservative academic institutions, while in the full-on, context-free competition engendered by EU's academic capitalism, an actual struggle seems to have broken out between the classical SSH-disciplines on the one hand and disciplines such as Neuroscience on the other hand. In order to gain further insight into these dynamics, we need to adopt a more sophisticated idea of what autonomy means, and how it is related to power relations in the context of field theory. Here we turn to the work of Schmitz et al. (2017), where a theoretical distinction is introduced between various types of dependence and independence from agents and resources inside as well as outside the given field. The logic of one field can act as a source of heteronomy in another, as seen, for example, in the case of market-oriented art or publicity-oriented politics. Because there are many social fields which interact in this way, various forms of autonomy K and heteronomy can exist. These need to be analyzed with the "field of power" in mind. With this concept, Bourdieu points to the overarching power structure in which all social fields are embedded and where each field occupies a more or less dominant or dominated position. The point here is that the struggle between autonomy and heteronomy inside a given field can only be understood if it is analyzed against this broader background of power relations inside as well as outside the field. We can apply these ideas for our purposes by 1. inquiring from whom/from what autonomous agents signal independence, and 2. by remembering that the discourse we examine is a result of strategies in an ongoing struggle for academic domination, which takes place in the context of various social fields, some of which are more powerful than others.

Even in the most autonomous academic practices, therefore, relative heteronomies remain among the structuring principles. They are also prevalent in the formulation of ERC Starting Grant summaries.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: In the following steps of our analysis, we attempt to shed more light on these questions, firstly by considering not only the objective positions in a topic space, but also the corresponding position-taking practices displayed by the individual Starting Grant projects. As discussed above, the project summaries we analyze are texts which attempt to speak to a number of diverse audiences at once. The three main addressees are 1. the scientific community, especially the ERC referees, 2. specific national and European bureaucratic and political institutions, and 3. the EU or Europe as the general funding context in which the research is situated.

In order to address the diverging expectations of these audiences in such a limited format, researchers have to find a compromise between signaling their purely academic intent towards the ERC referees, communicating the usefulness of their endeavor towards bureaucrats and politicians, and signaling compliance and support towards the EU and ERC science policy. In the following chapters, we examine three groups of such signals: First, we look at how researchers position their projects regarding the question of multi-, inter-and trans-disciplinarity. Then we ask whether specific beneficial outcomes are promised in the research summaries and if so, toward whom these benefits are directed. And finally, we examine the ways in which the research summaries refer to Europe or the EU, i. e. the new socio-political context which replaces the traditional framing of social sciences and humanities trough nation-states.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: As mentioned above, one key feature of the ERC funding strategy is that it attempts to subvert traditional distinctions in the academic world-e. g. the distinction between applied and basic research, as well as boundaries between disciplines-through the concept of "frontier research". Because of this, many project summaries contain passages which deal with the issue of disciplinary positioning, and especially with the question in what ways the project manages to "go beyond established frontiers of knowledge and the boundaries of disciplines" (Flink 2016, p. 310). The discussion of these issues, which is explicitly encouraged by the ERCfoot_1 , presents the opportunity for the researchers to speak to two audiences at once: The discussion of multi-and trans-disciplinarity enables the projects to position themselves in the spectrum of disciplines and sub-disciplines, which may be an important strategy to gain academic recognition, while simultaneously sending a signal of compliance with the values incorporated in ERC funding. The fact that some projects use the limited space of the summaries to position themselves in this way shows that they care very much about the role their research plays in the current academic landscape as well as the perception of their work by other scholars.
For example, one project in the field of religious studies is described as follows:

an interdisciplinary approach by bringing together Assyriologists and Biblical scholars for a much-needed dialogue, thereby exploding the artificial boundaries that currently exist in the academic landscape between these two fields

In this example, we see how the attempt to address two audiences is structured: While signaling compliance with the ERC strategy of subverting "artificial" boundaries in science, the project manages to clearly demarcate its position among specific sub-disciplines. In a similar way, another project describes itself as follows: both multi-disciplinary, engaging with [...] various fields contemporaneously, and inter-disciplinary, tackling problems in each field by using methodologies from others, and will have trans-disciplinary impact.

Another common statement is that through the project, a new disciplinary subfield is about to emerge: this radically interdisciplinary project aims to bring a substantially new field of research-literature and mathematics studies-to prominence as a tool for investigating the culture of nineteenth-century Britain A further common theme is that traditional practices in specific disciplines are modernized through the work of the projects. For example, one projects undertakes to Because relations between the various sub-disciplines discussed in these statements-most of which will only be familiar to specialists-are of purely academic concern, we interpret this type of statement as a signal of dependence on the scientific community. Projects which exhibit such signals care more strongly than others about the "correct" representation of their endeavor in the context of their discipline and about the recognition of their work by peers. In the formulations of Bourdieu, these projects could be designated as relatively autonomous in an academic sense, because they are more strongly bound to the logic of the academic field, and therefore have to disregard other competing demands and opportunities in their selfrepresentation as successful ERC projects.

Moving beyond individual examples, we ask where in the topic space such signals of academic autonomy are most common. Are specific disciplines more dependent on academic recognition than others? Do specific research topics cater more strongly to purely academic demands? Our aim in asking these questions is to discover whether the signals of autonomy discussed above can be interpreted as strategies in the underlying academic struggle: If so, they should be systematically related to the polarity of topics in our topic space and the underlying struggle between disciplines and paradigms. If however these were simply individual idiosyncrasies, there should be no systematic relationship between these signals and the position of the respective project in our topic space.
In order to assess this, we examine how positioning with regard to inter-multitrans-and cross-disciplinarity varies across the dimensions of the topic space. We consider two categories of projects:
1. those who explicitly mention issues of inter-, multi-, trans-or cross-disciplinarity in their abstract and 2. those who do not discuss these issues.

For both categories, we calculate the mean position along both dimensions of the topic space, along with the median and standard deviation. Further, we calculate t-tests for the category means. The results are displayed in Table 1.

Our results for the mean and median coordinates of these categories with regard to the horizontal dimension 1 reveal an interesting structure: Academic autonomy-as characterized by an explicit care for ones position in the disciplinary landscape-is expressed most strongly on the positive (right-hand) end of dimension 1, while the neglect of these issues is typical for projects on the opposite (left-hand) side. The median and the mean values are close together, which indicates a real difference between the groups. This finding is further strengthened by the high significance revealed in the t-test. With regard to the second, vertical dimension, the results presented in Table 1 show that there are no relevant or significant differences regarding this type of autonomy.

Thus, we can conclude that the practice of academic position-taking with regard to issues of disciplinary boundaries is a collective strategy in the struggle for academic recognition. While researchers in the cultural and historical fields tend to painstakingly signal their position towards academic peers, such a practice is uncommon in other areas of the topic space-and in other disciplines. Researchers in economics and related fields seem to be quite confident in the relevance of their discipline in the EU funding context, which might explain why they don't engage in similar academically-focused signaling practices. The strategy of signaling academic autonomy through a discussion of trans-, inter-and multi-disciplinarity is thus most strongly correlated with the right-hand side of the topic space and with cultural and historical research topics. With regards to our research question, it seems that the classical humanities stick to their traditional ways of symbolizing autonomy even in this transnational space governed by academic capitalism, where they have to compete directly with disciplines which contradict their very paradigm.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: Based on these results, we examine what types of impact researchers promise to generate through their ERC-funded work, and towards whom these benefits are directed: The scientific community or the society.

For example, there are a number of projects dealing with the promotion of democracy, with goals like "democratizing political decision-making". Many projects address problems in specific policy fields. In general, such projects promise to "contribute new knowledge to inform policies" and thus "provide timely insights to policy-makers facing the need to make strategic choices". Other motivations range between attempts to "minimize fundamentalist readings of the sacred texts", to "change our ideas of gender justice itself", or even to "improve semantic skills and creative thinking of healthy and impaired individuals". While the social issues targeted vary widely, there are nevertheless a number of dominant themes, including the clinical applicability of neuroscience, health, environmental issues as well as welfare, economic and financial policy. In many cases, such promises of an impact on society are coupled with claims of academic benefits, e. g. by claiming that the research "will not only be of scientific interest, but will also be of great value to K policymakers". Such claims can be seen as signals of compliance with the ERC's strategy of funding "frontier research", which aims to subvert the traditional distinction between applied and basic research.

In general, an attempt to promote a research project by explicitly promising beneficial outcomes for society can be seen as a sign of heteronomy: Research is not done for its own sake, according to the demands of the academic field, but needs to meet some external expectations. In projects which use this discursive strategy, academic concerns are overridden by attempts to signal compliance with the demands of agents outside the academic field, such as the welfare state, the EU, certain political initiatives or specific social groups. Therefore, we expect that claims of social impact will cluster on the heteronomous, left hand side of the topic space.
In order to assess this, we again employ our mode of analysis outlined in the preceding chapter. We consider four categories of projects:

1. those who do not explicitly discuss their impact at all, 2. those who claim that their research will lead to scientific advances, 3. those who promise academic as well as social impacts and 4. those who claim social impacts only.

The results are displayed in Table 2. If we look at the mean and median positions of these categories with regard to the horizontal dimension 1, a fairly clear picture emerges, which in many ways supports our findings from above: Academic autonomy-which is characterized by the absence of an explicit concern with impact, or by claims of academic impact only-is usually found on the positive (right-hand) end of dimension one, while claims of social impact (sometimes combined with academic impact) cluster on the opposite, negative (left-hand) end of the dimension. The standard deviation values indicate that there is a fairly wide variation in each of these categories: Promises of societal impact can and do occur among historians, philosophers and scholars of language, just as some economics projects are only focused on their contribution to science. Nevertheless, our analysis reveals strong tendencies for specific types of impact to be claimed in specific regions of the topic space. This finding is further K substantiated by the t-test. A similar analysis for dimension 2 reveals that this vertical dimension is largely irrelevant for the question of social or academic impact. The category means differ only very slightly and cluster around the centroid; the t-test reveals no significant differences.

Thus, we can conclude that the strategy of self-promotion through claims of a specific societal impact is systematically employed according to the logic of the ERC context. On the left hand side of the topic space, projects indicate academic heteronomy-understood as a strong regard for non-academic demands of utility and impact-while on the right hand side, projects promise only benefits for the scientific community or are completely unconcerned with the question of impact.

These findings underscore the results from the previous chapter: Dimension 1 should be regarded as the major dimension of academic autonomy and heteronomy, with the heteronomous pole-dominated by extra-academic references-at its left hand side, and the autonomous pole at its right hand side. Both of these poles are associated with opposing discursive strategies of self-presentation: Research agendas focused on economy and finance actively cater to the perceived demands of society, while historical research remains politically aloof and addresses itself only to a scholarly audience. Political science and psychology would then occupy a middle position between these two extremes.

In the light of these results, the idea that there might exist not only different degrees of autonomy, but different types of autonomy, each focused on specific structuring principles present in the SSH, becomes plausible. With regard to dimension 1, two such types of autonomy seem to exist:
1. Autonomy from extra-academic demands on the right and 2. autonomy from the demands of the academic field itself on the left, with the latter thus being 'academically heteronomous'.

But-as Bourdieu reminds us with his concept of the field of power-these two types of autonomy don't stand on equal footing: Among all the social fields, the academic field belongs to the dominated group, while the economic, political and bureaucratic fields hold more power in society, with their respective structuring principles far more influential (cp. Schmitz et al. 2017). Academic conflicts such as the one observed here are acted out in the context of this power structure: Those disciplines which gain their status exclusively from the academic field might be academically more autonomous, but they are still dominated by disciplines which abide with the structuring principles of the most powerful fields.

Therefore, it would seem that the classical humanities should be in a rather weak position given the general direction of EU science policy, and the fact that competing disciplines aggressively claim the ability to improve policy and society with science. How can we explain the strong prevalence of symbolically aloof, impactfree historical and cultural research topics in an institutional climate of academic capitalism? sections.text:
Based on these results, we examine what types of impact researchers promise to generate through their ERC-funded work, and towards whom these benefits are directed: The scientific community or the society.

For example, there are a number of projects dealing with the promotion of democracy, with goals like "democratizing political decision-making". Many projects address problems in specific policy fields. In general, such projects promise to "contribute new knowledge to inform policies" and thus "provide timely insights to policy-makers facing the need to make strategic choices". Other motivations range between attempts to "minimize fundamentalist readings of the sacred texts", to "change our ideas of gender justice itself", or even to "improve semantic skills and creative thinking of healthy and impaired individuals". While the social issues targeted vary widely, there are nevertheless a number of dominant themes, including the clinical applicability of neuroscience, health, environmental issues as well as welfare, economic and financial policy. In many cases, such promises of an impact on society are coupled with claims of academic benefits, e. g. by claiming that the research "will not only be of scientific interest, but will also be of great value to K policymakers". Such claims can be seen as signals of compliance with the ERC's strategy of funding "frontier research", which aims to subvert the traditional distinction between applied and basic research.

In general, an attempt to promote a research project by explicitly promising beneficial outcomes for society can be seen as a sign of heteronomy: Research is not done for its own sake, according to the demands of the academic field, but needs to meet some external expectations. In projects which use this discursive strategy, academic concerns are overridden by attempts to signal compliance with the demands of agents outside the academic field, such as the welfare state, the EU, certain political initiatives or specific social groups. Therefore, we expect that claims of social impact will cluster on the heteronomous, left hand side of the topic space.

In order to assess this, we again employ our mode of analysis outlined in the preceding chapter. We consider four categories of projects:

1. those who do not explicitly discuss their impact at all, 2. those who claim that their research will lead to scientific advances, 3. those who promise academic as well as social impacts and 4. those who claim social impacts only.

The results are displayed in Table 2. If we look at the mean and median positions of these categories with regard to the horizontal dimension 1, a fairly clear picture emerges, which in many ways supports our findings from above: Academic autonomy-which is characterized by the absence of an explicit concern with impact, or by claims of academic impact only-is usually found on the positive (right-hand) end of dimension one, while claims of social impact (sometimes combined with academic impact) cluster on the opposite, negative (left-hand) end of the dimension. The standard deviation values indicate that there is a fairly wide variation in each of these categories: Promises of societal impact can and do occur among historians, philosophers and scholars of language, just as some economics projects are only focused on their contribution to science. Nevertheless, our analysis reveals strong tendencies for specific types of impact to be claimed in specific regions of the topic space. This finding is further K substantiated by the t-test. A similar analysis for dimension 2 reveals that this vertical dimension is largely irrelevant for the question of social or academic impact. The category means differ only very slightly and cluster around the centroid; the t-test reveals no significant differences.

Thus, we can conclude that the strategy of self-promotion through claims of a specific societal impact is systematically employed according to the logic of the ERC context. On the left hand side of the topic space, projects indicate academic heteronomy-understood as a strong regard for non-academic demands of utility and impact-while on the right hand side, projects promise only benefits for the scientific community or are completely unconcerned with the question of impact.

These findings underscore the results from the previous chapter: Dimension 1 should be regarded as the major dimension of academic autonomy and heteronomy, with the heteronomous pole-dominated by extra-academic references-at its left hand side, and the autonomous pole at its right hand side. Both of these poles are associated with opposing discursive strategies of self-presentation: Research agendas focused on economy and finance actively cater to the perceived demands of society, while historical research remains politically aloof and addresses itself only to a scholarly audience. Political science and psychology would then occupy a middle position between these two extremes.

In the light of these results, the idea that there might exist not only different degrees of autonomy, but different types of autonomy, each focused on specific structuring principles present in the SSH, becomes plausible. With regard to dimension 1, two such types of autonomy seem to exist:

1. Autonomy from extra-academic demands on the right and 2. autonomy from the demands of the academic field itself on the left, with the latter thus being 'academically heteronomous'.

But-as Bourdieu reminds us with his concept of the field of power-these two types of autonomy don't stand on equal footing: Among all the social fields, the academic field belongs to the dominated group, while the economic, political and bureaucratic fields hold more power in society, with their respective structuring principles far more influential (cp. Schmitz et al. 2017). Academic conflicts such as the one observed here are acted out in the context of this power structure: Those disciplines which gain their status exclusively from the academic field might be academically more autonomous, but they are still dominated by disciplines which abide with the structuring principles of the most powerful fields.

Therefore, it would seem that the classical humanities should be in a rather weak position given the general direction of EU science policy, and the fact that competing disciplines aggressively claim the ability to improve policy and society with science. How can we explain the strong prevalence of symbolically aloof, impactfree historical and cultural research topics in an institutional climate of academic capitalism? In the following chapter, we examine how signals of autonomy from the European funding context are related to this question.
