sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
sections.header_raw: References to europe and the european union
sections.header_norm: references to europe and the european union
paragraphs: Having established how the topic space is modeled by structuring principles from within the SSH as well as from the powerful fields of politics and bureaucracy, our last step is to see whether the research projects signal compliance towards the transnational context in which they are funded, by somehow referencing or incorporating the implied values of the EU research policy or the European idea in general. Heteronomy in this sense means that research projects are claimed to be especially relevant for the European context-for example by carrying out research about the political issues relevant to the EU or on the cultural heritage of Europe. Autonomy in this context means that the researchers don't display any explicit regard or orientation toward the European idea or the EU.

As outlined in the first chapter, the humanities and social sciences have historically been characterized by their particular nation-state context and their function has been to stabilize, legitimize and explicate the character of nation-states in sociological, political, economic, cultural and psychological terms. The question therefore is whether in the transnational ERC context, this affinity towards the nation-state is somehow transformed-or even replaced by a similar regard for Europe and the EU: To what degree do the projects display autonomy or heteronomy with regard to the ERC funding context and the values and institutions of the EU? To what extent do nation-states feature in the European transnational context? About 25% of the project summaries make some kind of reference towards the European Union or Europe; references to nation-states are much less common and frequently appear in conjunction with a reference toward the EU or Europe. 5 These references vary in the specific way in which they seek to make themselves relevant in the European funding context. The maximum degree of heteronomy can be observed in projects which explicitly present issues regarding the EU or Europe as the motivating force behind their research. For example:

This project uses an innovative anthropological approach to study gambling as a social and cultural activity, and uses these findings to establish a new research paradigm that is technologically astute, internationally focused and aggressively future oriented. Gambling in Europe is worth an estimated [C]89 billion and is a rapidly expanding and changing industry. It is also a source of concern to legislators and consumers. Gambling legislation is not harmonised at the European level. National governments are currently moving at varying speeds between containment and revenue generation [...] Like legislators, the research community has failed to keep pace with these changes and continues to focus on quantifying and categorising gamblers and gambling activities within national boundaries.

In this project summary, gambling is presented as an important concern for Europe and the EU, and the project promises to help gambling policy to transcend its inefficient national fragmentation. The research itself is depicted as relevant precisely because of its potential to inform politics and legislation, while academic concerns are presented as secondary.
Another example of this strategy is the following:
One of the greatest contemporary challenges for the social sciences is to understand the human costs of the ongoing financial, economic, and debt crises that began to emerge in Europe in 2007.

Here, Europe is presented in a hegemonic position, so that the relevance of research topics in the social sciences is dictated by Europe's current political and economic difficulties. Academic practice is depicted as subservient to concerns of Europe, and merely charged with an analysis of the social consequences ("human cost") of EU market-integration.

A slightly less aggressive approach to self-promotion uses Europe or the EU as an added bonus of relevance for research which is primarily motivated by academic concerns. In this strategy, Europe is tacked on, so to speak, to research which could just as well be conducted in another context. Projects vary in how much effort they put into concealing this tacked-on character of Europe. A relatively obvious example can be seen in a project which deals with ethno-religious conflicts in Nigeria. Europe is only mentioned in the last sentence of the project abstract:

[The project aims to enhance] the theoretical and practical understanding of religious tolerance, both in Nigeria and in other countries in which religious difference is politicised, including Europe.

In these cases, references to Europe are inserted into presentations of research as an afterthought which has little intrinsic relation to Europe, in order to demonstrate concern for Europe and compliance toward the implied values of the ERC context.

An even weaker form of heteronomy is exhibited by projects which aim to transfer already established research traditions onto the European level, for example by publishing books, organizing workshops, building websites and data archives which are accessible to all scholars in Europe. Another variation is to simply highlight the fact that the research is done in Europe and therefore contributes to Europe's scientific reputation, for example by promising "a ground-breaking contribution to European research on the Arabic language".

Autonomy from the European context, on the other hand, is evident in projects where such signals of compliance are missing, and where the research goals are stated in a universalist mode which highlights context-invariance. This is most typical for projects in psychology and neuroscience, where projects are motivated by questions and statements like:

When is "now"? What mental representations and neural computations mediate the construction of our perceived present? The ability to control our social behaviour is essential for almost every social interaction. It frequently fails in challenging situations when people fall back on basic defensive freeze-fight-flight (FFF) reactions. To be sure, the universalist and objectivist modes of presentation by no means precludes references to Europe: Research on freeze-fight-flight reactions for example could just as well be linked to current political or social issues in Europe as the investigation of medieval troubadours or Central-African religious politics. The fact that such references are not made is a sign of autonomy from the specific concerns and values of the ERC funding context.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: In order to assess whether such references to nation-states, Europe or the EU can be interpreted as collective strategies in the competition for ERC funds, we again use the methodology of the previous chapters. In this case, the categories are 1. projects with a reference to a nation-state, 2. projects with a reference to the EU or Europe, 3. projects with reference to both nation-state and Europe and 4. projects where no such references are made.

If we look at the mean and median positions of these categories with regard to the two dimensions of our topic space (Table 3), an interesting structure emerges: Autonomy from the specific funding context of the ERC-which is characterized by the absence of references toward Europe or the EU-is found mainly on the negative (bottom) end of dimension 2, i. e. in the lower half of the topic space, in the region governed by psychological, neuro-scientific and linguistic research. On the other hand, the further we move up towards the other disciplines, the more we find references relating to Europe and the EU-signs of heteronomy regarding the funding context. At the top of dimension two, in the area governed by research into international and global issues and history, European references are occasionally coupled with references toward specific nation-states.

With regard to dimension 1-which featured so prominently in our previous analyses-the category means do not differ significantly, except for the category of combined references to Europe and the nation-state, which are situated to the right hand side of the topic space.

sections.preview_tail: With regard to dimension 1-which featured so prominently in our previous analyses-the category means do not differ significantly, except for the category of combined references to Europe and the nation-state, which are situated to the right hand side of the topic space.

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: References to nation-states alone don't occur in a specific location in the topic space, neither regarding dimension 1 nor dimension 2. Although some project summaries mention nation-states, there is no systematic correspondence to specific research themes or disciplines and no clear position of such references in the topic space. Therefore, referencing nation states can't be interpreted as a specific strategy in the competition for ERC funds.

In contrast, references to Europe are significantly tied to specific areas in the topic space and can therefore be seen as a strategy in the ongoing struggle for academic prestige. This finding shows that in the transnational ERC context, Europe begins to play the role of the socio-political point of orientation in the SSH, which traditionally has been occupied by nation-states. This result is underscored by the fact that combined references to nation-states and Europe are also significant and are positioned at the heteronomous pole of dimension 1.

Thus, we can conclude that in the lower regions of the topic space, projects exhibit a second type of autonomy, which may be understood as a relative indifference towards the European funding context and its implied values. In contrast, in the upper regions of the topic space, projects promote themselves by referring to issues which appear relevant given the European funding context, and thus exhibit compliance with the perceived demands and preoccupations of Europe and the EU. Dimension 2 can therefore be regarded as the dimension of autonomy or heteronomy regarding the specific funding context.

With regard to our research question, we see that the transnational context of the ERC indeed changes the ways in which the SSH describe themselves: The traditional focus on nation-state-specific themes is quite thoroughly displaced by a focus on European themes. If this amounts to a Europeanization of the SSH, it is a rather limited one, because only the content is exchanged, while the general logic of heteronomy from a locus of social power-so characteristic for these disciplines-lives on in the transnational arena. Notably, deference to implied demands of Europe and the EU is strongest in the upper-right region of our topic space, where the classical disciplines of the humanities are located. In the chapter above, we raised the question as to how these disciplines could become so prominent in spite of their relative weakness with regard to the ERC's criteria for scientific excellence. In the light of our findings, we propose that the humanities succeed on the European scale with the same strategy which they employed in their early days vis-à-vis the emerging nation states, i. e. through a self-promotion focused on autonomy and aloofness, and the willingness to investigate-and thereby construct and consecrate-the history and culture of Europe. Through this strategy, ERC-funded research in the humanities adds an important ingredient to the process of Europeanization: Once European culture is recognized as a legitimate theme of academic discourse, the politicized doubts about the existence of such a culture can be put to rest. This, it seems, is the reason why the classical humanities manage to be so successful in the transnational ERC arena.

With regard to the pole of autonomy from the European context, we can assume that the ability of projects in neuro-science and psychology to credibly embody a universalist and objectivist stance stems from their affinity towards the life sciences and natural sciences. Further investigation will be necessary in order to inquire into K the ways in which this type of autonomy functions, because what we perceive as relative autonomy in the given context might actually be based on another form of heteronomy, namely dependence on recognition from-and orientation towards-the so-called "hard sciences".

sections.paper_id: Baier and Gengnagel - 2018 - Academic Autonomy Beyond the Nation-State The Social Sciences and Humanities in the European Resear
paragraphs: The aim of this paper was to show how successful ERC Starting Grant projects in the SSH signal academic autonomy and heteronomy in their self-representation, and how these discursive strategies are shaped by the transnational European arena in which they appear. To this end, we conducted a combined qualitative and quantitative text analysis on a dataset of 365 project summaries of successful Starting Grant projects in the SSH. A topic model with 20 topics was constructed, which revealed a hierarchy of research themes, with historical and neuro-scientific topics at the top, closely followed by topics from economics, political science and law. Other topics, e. g. religion, and other disciplines, e. g. sociology, feature less prominently.

On the basis of the correlations between topics, we constructed a latent topic space which illustrates the distinctions and alliances between research themes, disciplines and strategies of self-promotion. The topic space is structured around four clusters, each comprising interrelated research themes and discursive practices. We analyzed the structure of the topic space along the lines of Bourdieu's theory of the academic field: Strategies of self-representation in ERC-funded SSH research are part of an underlying struggle for academic power. The main line of contention in this struggle is the opposition between researchers posing as pure agents of the academic ethos, autonomous from non-academic influences, and researchers who present their project as heteronomous, i. e. explicitly tailored toward political, bureaucratic or other nonacademic concerns.

We examined three discursive manifestations of this opposition: Disciplinary positioning, promises of societal impact and signals of compliance toward the EU or the nation state. The four clusters of topics differ systematically in the way they relate to these issues: Historical and cultural research generally doesn't claim societal impact but is very much concerned about its position in the academic realm, and emphasizes its specific relevance for Europe and the EU. Research in economics, in contrast, tends not to indicate academic alliances, but routinely claims relevance for societal problems, and also makes strong references to the EU. The dimension between these two clusters represents the classical conflict between academic autonomy and heteronomy-dependence and independence from powers outside academe-which was already described by Bourdieu (1992), drawing on Kant's Conflict of the Faculties (1996Faculties ( [1798]]). As this distinction is played out in a transnational arena beyond the entrenched structures of national academic fields, it can be expected that those who are able to speak about the issues of politics and bureaucracy will displace those who can't or won't touch such issues. Nevertheless, we see that history and related disciplines of the humanities are very prominent in the ERC-funded SSH. According to our theory, the reason may be that these disciplines manage to gain their strong position by investing their high symbolic capital into the academic analysis, construction and consecration of European culture and history.

Another dimension of this struggle is manifest in the distinction between those who align themselves explicitly with the implied values of the European arena and those who take a universalist, politically aloof stance by not acknowledging this specific funding context. In disciplines like political science, law, history and economics, it is common practice to send clear signs of interest and compliance toward the European idea and the political concerns of the EU. In contrast, research in psychology and neuro-science is typically promoted in a universalist mode, indifferent to Europe and the specific EU context of funding.

These findings show that through the transnationalization of SSH discourse in the ERC arena, the nation state is demoted from its historical position as the sociopolitical horizon of academic practice in the SSH. In its stead, Europe and the EU are erected as new symbolic reference points, and the latent effects of legitimization and consecration of these disciplines now help stabilize the European idea. This means that the move of the SSH into a new transnational arena does not imply a change in the basic structures of dependence and distinction which govern scientific work in the SSH. Just as on the national level, large parts of the SSH display a peculiar sensitivity towards the values and needs implied by the specific socio-political context they find themselves in, and are readily able to adapt their focus as this context is shifted to the European level. Strikingly, such a sensitivity is quite absent in disciplines like psychology and neuro-science, which may be due to the affinity of these disciplines to the natural sciences.

Our findings raise two main questions which should be addressed in further research: Firstly, the peculiar role of psychology and neuro-science as challengers in the field of the SSH should be investigated further, so that our findings, which center on the SSH, can be placed in the larger context of the changing power relations in science. Secondly, in showing how patterns of compliant self-representation and elitist facade permeate the ERC-funded SSH discourse, our analysis raises the question how these discursive phenomena are related to actual scientific practice and the felt self-identities of scientists and scholars. Further research should seek to elucidate the various modes of colonization, mimicry or decoupling that emerge as researchers attempt to align their academic illusio with the structural imperatives of the European sphere.
